title_en: The Unseen: Angels, the Day of Judgment, and Matters Beyond Perception
title_ar: الغيب: الملائكة ويوم القيامة والأمور الخارجة عن الإدراك
content_en:
A defining characteristic of Salafi creed is the approach to matters of the unseen (al-ghayb) — realities that exist beyond the reach of human perception and that are known only through divine revelation. The Salaf accepted all such matters as the Quran and authentic Sunnah described them, without rationalistic reinterpretation, allegorical deflection, or demand for experiential verification. This chapter covers the major matters of the unseen that Salafi creed addresses: the angels, the details of the afterlife, the events preceding the Day of Judgment, and the realities of the grave.

The Salaf accepted the existence of the angels (mala'ikah) as real beings created from light, endowed with consciousness and will, who execute divine commands without disobedience. They affirmed the specific roles attributed to individual angels in the Quran and Sunnah: Jibril as the deliverer of revelation, Israfil as the blower of the trumpet, Mika'il as responsible for rain, Munkar and Nakir as the questioners in the grave, Malik as the guardian of Hellfire, and Ridwan as the guardian of Paradise. The Salaf did not allegorize the angels as psychological forces or metaphors; they affirmed them as genuinely existing beings in the unseen realm.

On the afterlife, the Salafi creed as documented from the early scholars includes: the reality of the questioning in the grave by Munkar and Nakir, the punishment of the grave for the disobedient, and the comfort of the grave for the righteous. The Salaf affirmed these on the basis of mutawatir-level hadith evidence, despite the fact that the grave's punishment is not perceived by those present at a burial. The principle is clear: what is affirmed by authentic Sunnah at the level of mass transmission is accepted without requiring additional rational proof.

The major events of the Day of Judgment are affirmed as they are described: the blowing of the trumpet, the universal death and resurrection, the gathering (hashr) of all creation, the presentation of deeds (the scales — al-mizan), the bridge over Hell (as-sirat), the intercession (shafa'ah) of the Prophet and other intercessors by Allah's permission, the pool (hawd) of the Prophet from which the believers will drink, and the final entry of the believers into Paradise and the disbelievers into Hellfire — an eternal abode for each.

The Salaf held a specific and carefully documented position on the major signs of the Hour: the emergence of the Dajjal, the descent of Isa ibn Maryam from the heavens, the emergence of Ya'juj and Ma'juj, the rising of the sun from the west, the beast of the earth (ad-dabbah), and the smoke (ad-dukhan). These are affirmed based on the mass-transmitted hadith literature and are not to be allegorized or dismissed as mythological.

Particularly relevant to the Salafi tradition is the affirmation of the prophetic intercession (shafa'ah). The Salaf affirmed the intercession of the Prophet on the Day of Judgment for the people of major sins, as well as other forms of intercession. This directly contradicted the Mu'tazilah, who denied that the doers of major sins would receive intercession. The Salafi position: intercession is established by the Quran and Sunnah, it is Allah's permission to the intercessor and His acceptance of the plea — not an independent power exercised by the intercessor. This is why affirming intercession does not constitute shirk, whereas supplicating to the Prophet or other intercessors as if they had independent power to help does.

The chapter closes with the methodological principle that governs all of this: in matters of the unseen, the Salaf said 'we believe and we accept,' and did not demand rational explanations for how these things would occur. The resurrection of the dead, for example, is known to be possible for the One Who created them the first time — further rational elaboration adds nothing.
